provenance.title: Scenario Generation for Time Series and Curves: A Comparison of Nonparametric and Semiparametric Bootstrap
provenance.abstract: Generating stochastic trajectories for asset classes is an increasingly relevant task in quantitative finance. Traditional approaches, such as the stationary bootstrap, preserve by construction the empirical distribution of asset-class returns, but do not ensure that each simulated path is economically realistic. We review semiparametric methods combining parametric structure with resampling of residuals.
key_claims: The Stationary Bootstrap preserves empirical marginal distributions asymptotically but does not guarantee individual simulated paths are economically realistic.
The VAR-Bootstrap introduces an autoregressive (mean-reverting) structure combined with nonparametric residual resampling, trading some distributional fidelity for dynamic/economic plausibility.

Embedding a dynamic Nelson-Siegel factor representation into the VAR-Bootstrap (Nelson-Siegel VAR-Bootstrap) yields more regular and financially coherent simulated yield curves than resampling rates directly.

Empirically, Stationary Bootstrap has the lowest KL divergence to historical distributions, but VAR-Bootstrap and NS VAR-Bootstrap substantially reduce no-arbitrage (monotonicity of discount factor) violations.
No single methodology dominates: the choice is a trade-off between statistical adherence to history and economic-financial plausibility of individual paths.

modules.name: StationaryBootstrap
modules.input_shape: [T, n] historical joint vector z_t = (r_t, Δy_t)

parameters.resampled_quantities: price returns r_t and rate first-differences Δy_t

modules.notes: Baseline/reference method. Levels reconstructed multiplicatively for price factors (P_hat_t = (1+r*_t) ⊙ P_hat_{t-1}) and additively for rate factors (y_hat_t = y_hat_{t-1} + Δy*_t).

modules.name: VARBootstrap
modules.input_shape: [T, n] state vector x_t = (r_t, y_t), n = n_x + n_y

parameters.model: VAR(1): x_t = a0 + A1 x_{t-1} + eta_t
parameters.residual_treatment: nonparametric resampling with replacement of historical residuals eta_hat_t

modules.notes: Price component represented as returns r_t, rate component as levels y_t. Equity levels reconstructed multiplicatively post-simulation; rate levels taken directly from x_hat_t. Parameter count grows as n^2 (A1 matrix).

modules.name: NelsonSiegelFactorExtraction
modules.input_shape: [T, M] observed rates y_t(tau) across M maturities

parameters.lambda_calibration: exogenous; chosen by minimizing MSE between observed and reconstructed rates using maturities <= 5 years, fixed across time
parameters.per_date_estimation: quadratic (least-squares) optimization of beta0, beta1, beta2 for each date given fixed lambda

modules.notes: Level/slope/curvature interpretation of beta0/beta1/beta2. Follows dynamic Diebold-Li (2006) parameterization of static Nelson-Siegel (1987).

modules.name: NelsonSiegelVARBootstrap
modules.input_shape: [T, 3] Nelson-Siegel factor series beta_t (optionally augmented with other price/rate factors r_t, y_t in joint state x_t)
modules.output_shape: [T, M] simulated yield curve y_hat_t(tau) across maturities tau

parameters.model: VAR(1) on factors: beta_t = a0 + A1 beta_{t-1} + eta_t
parameters.residual_treatment: nonparametric resampling with replacement of historical factor-VAR residuals
parameters.curve_reconstruction: y_hat_t(tau) = ell(tau) beta_hat_t, with ell(tau) = [1, L1(tau), L2(tau)]
parameters.fitting_error_treatment: epsilon_t(tau) (per-maturity NS fitting residual) is NOT resampled; only factor dynamics are propagated

modules.notes: Mitigates the n^2 parameter growth of plain VAR-Bootstrap applied directly to all curve nodes by modeling only 3 latent factors. Joint state vector can include additional price returns r_t and non-curve level factors y_t alongside beta_t.

differences.state: x_t = (r_t, y_t) with y_t possibly the full set of curve maturities

differences.type: semiparametric VAR(1) on 3 latent NS factors + residual bootstrap + curve reconstruction
differences.state: beta_t = (beta0_t, beta1_t, beta2_t), optionally augmented with r_t, y_t

mathematical_spec.name: Discount factor / no-arbitrage monotonicity condition
mathematical_spec.latex: B(0,\tau) = e^{-\tau y(\tau)}, \qquad 1 = B(0,0) \geq B(0,T_1) \geq B(0,T_2) \geq 0 \text{ for } T_1 < T_2

optimizer.name: Ordinary Least Squares (VAR(1) estimation) / per-date quadratic optimization (Nelson-Siegel factor fit)

datasets.name: MSCI Europe Net Total Return EUR Index (Equity EUR)
12-month Italian Treasury Bills (BOT) gross yield, proxy for 1y Italian rate
Euro area HICP ex-tobacco (non-revised, non-seasonally adjusted)

metrics: Mean value of negative increments (conditional intensity)
Kullback-Leibler divergence (historical vs simulated marginal distributions)
Share of simulated curves with non-negative rates at all maturities
Percentage of no-arbitrage (discount factor monotonicity) violations
special_conditions: 10^4 simulated trajectories per methodology, 5-year horizon (1260 daily nodes or 60 monthly observations)
Inflation modeled only at monthly frequency with long-run level constrained to 2%
Yield curve analysis restricted to scenarios with non-negative rates at all maturities when testing arbitrage violations

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Stationary Bootstrap average block length / geometric distribution parameter is not explicitly given in the paper; a literature-typical value (e.g., mean block length ~ optimal per Politis-Romano rule or a fixed value such as 20 trading days) must be assumed.
implementation_assumptions.basis: literature default (Politis & Romano 1994 optimal block-length heuristics are standard practice)
alternatives: Data-driven optimal block length selection (Politis & White 2004)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Number and choice of historical sample window (start/end dates) for estimating VAR(1) and Nelson-Siegel parameters is not stated; assume the full available history for each series up to the paper's writing (mid-2026).
implementation_assumptions.basis: common practice for backtesting scenario generators

implementation_assumptions.assumption: VAR(1) parameters (a0, A1) estimated via ordinary least squares equation-by-equation, which is the standard/efficient estimator for VAR models under i.i.d. or weakly dependent residuals.
implementation_assumptions.basis: standard econometric practice for VAR(1) estimation
alternatives: Maximum likelihood estimation (equivalent to OLS under Gaussian errors)
GLS if residuals are correlated across equations

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Nelson-Siegel decay parameter lambda is calibrated once (not time-varying) by minimizing MSE over the full history using maturities <= 5y, then held fixed for factor extraction at every date.
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicitly stated in Section 4, but the exact numeric value of lambda and optimizer (e.g., grid search vs. Nelder-Mead) are not given
alternatives: Grid search over a lambda range
Fixed conventional lambda value (e.g., 0.0609 as in Diebold-Li 2006, tuned to maximize curvature loading at ~30 months)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: KDE bandwidth for the Kullback-Leibler divergence computation is not specified; assume a standard rule-of-thumb bandwidth selector (e.g., Silverman's or Scott's rule).
implementation_assumptions.basis: paper explicitly notes KL value depends on bandwidth choice but does not specify it

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Residual resampling for VAR-Bootstrap and NS-VAR-Bootstrap draws i.i.d. with replacement from the full historical residual set (not block resampling), consistent with the AR-Bootstrap illustration in Appendix A.
implementation_assumptions.basis: inferred from Appendix A worked example, which resamples individual residuals with replacement rather than blocks
alternatives: Block bootstrap of residuals to preserve residual autocorrelation/heteroskedasticity
Parametric (e.g., Gaussian) residual simulation instead of resampling

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Implementation language/framework not specified by the paper; assume Python with numpy/pandas/statsmodels/scipy as the standard quant research stack.
implementation_assumptions.basis: common practice in quantitative finance research reproduction

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Maturities used for the full yield-curve analysis are 3m, 6m, and annual points from 1y to 10y (12 points total), as explicitly listed in Section 8 / Table 9.

ambiguities.description: The exact block-length distribution parameter (mean block length) used for the geometric-block stationary bootstrap is never given numerically.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Optimal block length via Politis-Romano-style heuristic, order ~sqrt(T) to T^(1/3) trading days

ambiguities.description: The specific numeric value obtained for the exogenous decay parameter lambda after MSE minimization is not reported anywhere in the paper.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: lambda close to the Diebold-Li canonical value (~0.06 per month equivalent) but re-fit for this dataset
alternatives: Any lambda in a plausible 0.02-0.15 monthly-equivalent range depending on units of tau

ambiguities.description: KDE bandwidth selection method for computing the Kullback-Leibler divergence is explicitly acknowledged by the authors as consequential but is not specified.

Whether VAR-Bootstrap residuals are resampled independently at each simulated time step (i.i.d.) or in blocks (to preserve any residual autocorrelation/volatility clustering) is not explicitly stated in the main text; only the simple worked AR(1) example in Appendix A resamples i.i.d.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: i.i.d. resampling with replacement from the pooled historical residual set

ambiguities.description: Exact start/end dates of the historical estimation sample for Equity EUR, the 1y Italian rate, and inflation are not given.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: A multi-decade sample ending at/near the paper's writing date (2026), long enough to cover multiple rate cycles
alternatives: A shorter post-2008 or post-2015 sample
A sample restricted to a specific monetary-policy regime
